turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: In case 16-1027, Collins v. Virginia, Justice Sotomayor has the opinion for the Court.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: This case presents the question whether the automobile exception to the Fourth Amendment permits a police officer, uninvited and without a warrant, to intrude upon the curtilage of a home to search a vehicle parked therein. We hold that it does not. David Rhodes, a police officer in Virginia, was investigating two traffic incidents involving a motorcyclist after Officer Rhodes learned that the motorcycle likely was stolen and in the possession of petitioner Ryan Collins. He went to Collins' house. From his position on the street he saw what appeared to be a motorcycle under a white tarp near the top of the driveway. Acting without a search warrant Officer Rhodes walked onto the property and up to the motorcycle. He removed the tarp to reveal the license plate and Vehicle Identification Numbers and ran a search of those numbers to confirm that the motorcycle was stolen. He then waited for Collins to return home and arrested him for receiving stolen property. Collins filed a motion to suppress, arguing that Officer Rhodes had trespassed on his curtilage to conduct an investigation in violation of the Fourth Amendment. The Trial Court denied the motion and the Court of Appeals of Virginia affirmed, as did the Supreme Court of Virginia, which reasoned that the search was justified pursuant to the automobile exception. This case arises at the intersection of two aspects of our Fourth Amendment Jurisprudence, the automobile exception and the protection extended to the curtilage of a house. As to the first, the Court has held that the search of an automobile can be reasonable without a warrant because of the readily mobility of automobiles and the pervasive regulation of vehicles capable of traveling on public highways. In announcing these rationales the Court emphasized that they apply only to automobiles and not to houses. As to the second, the Court has long made clear that curtilage, the area immediately surrounding and associated with the home, is considered part of the home itself for Fourth Amendment purposes. An intrusion on the curtilage by a law enforcement officer to gather evidence is a search and such conduct is presumptively unreasonable without a warrant. For reasons we explained in our opinion the part of the driveway where Collins' motorcycle was searched is curtilage. Virginia contends that Officer Rhodes' intrusion on the curtilage to search the motorcycle was justified by the automobile exception. We disagree. The scope of the automobile exception extends no further than the automobile itself. It does not give an officer the right to enter a home or its curtilage to access a vehicle without a warrant. The Court has similarly declined to expand the scope of other exceptions to the warrant requirement and that logic applies equally well here. Under the Plain View Doctrine, an officer must have lawful right of access to any contraband he discovers in plain view in order to seize it without a warrant. Likewise, although warrantless arrests in public places are valid, an officer generally may not enter a home to make an arrest without a warrant. So too an officer must have a lawful right of access to a vehicle in order to search it pursuant to the automobile exception. The automobile exception itself does not afford that necessary right of access because the rationales underlying it take account only of the balance between the intrusion of an individual's Fourth Amendment interests in his vehicle and the government's interest in an expedient search of that vehicle. They do not account for the distinct privacy interests in one's home or curtilage. Virginia argues that the court's precedent indicates that the automobile exception is a categorical one that permits the warrantless search of a vehicle anytime, anywhere, but that the precedents on which it relies are neither dispositive, nor persuasive. Alternatively, Virginia urges a compromise position, which is that the automobile exception permits warrantless entry into some types of cartilage, but not into a fixed enclosed structure inside the curtilage or into the home. This carve out approach however is likely to create confusion for officers, is rooted in a mistaken premise about the significance of visual observation and deprives those persons without the financial means to afford garages of any individualized determination as to whether the areas in which they store their vehicles qualify as curtilage. For these and other reasons set forth in our opinion, we reverse the judgment of the Supreme Court of Virginia and remand for further proceedings not inconsistent with this opinion. Justice Thomas has filed a concurring opinion. Justice Alito has filed a dissenting opinion.
